data.title: Internal Combustion Engine
data.previewSummary: Engine that generates power through combustion of fuel and air.
previewBullets: Combustion chamber ignites fuel and air mixture
Piston moves up and down to generate power
Crankshaft converts up and down motion to rotary motion
data.details:
Combustion chamber ignites fuel and air mixture, producing high pressure and temperature. Piston moves up and down, driven by explosive force. Crankshaft converts up and down motion to rotary motion, generating power. 
Engine efficiency depends on combustion chamber design, fuel type, and air-fuel mixture.
data.analogy: Internal combustion engine is like a heart, pumping power to the body.
mechanism: Fuel and air mixture enters combustion chamber
Ignition source ignites mixture, producing high pressure and temperature
Piston moves up and down, driven by explosive force
misconceptions: Internal combustion engine is more efficient than electric engine
Internal combustion engine is cleaner than electric engine
constraints: Limited efficiency due to heat loss
Dependence on fuel and air supply
data.problemPatterns: Engine efficiency and emissions are major concerns.
formalStructure: Linear relationship between fuel flow and power output
Exponential decay of engine efficiency with increasing temperature

data.title: Petrol Engine Basics
data.previewSummary: Petrol engines convert chemical energy into mechanical energy using a four-stroke cycle.
previewBullets: Air-fuel mixture is drawn into the cylinder
Fuel is ignited, causing a small explosion
Piston moves, turning the crankshaft
Crankshaft converts motion into rotational energy
data.details:
Petrol engines use a four-stroke cycle: intake, compression, power, and exhaust. Air-fuel mixture is drawn into the cylinder, compressed, and ignited. The resulting explosion pushes the piston down, turning the crankshaft. The crankshaft converts this motion into rotational energy, which is transmitted to the wheels.
This process is repeated in multiple cylinders, creating a continuous flow of power.
data.analogy: A petrol engine is like a tiny, efficient factory that converts chemical energy into mechanical energy.
mechanism: Air-fuel mixture is drawn into the cylinder
Fuel is ignited, causing a small explosion
Piston moves, turning the crankshaft
misconceptions: Petrol engines are only used in cars
formalStructure: Linear relationship between fuel flow and power output
Exponential decay of engine efficiency with temperature
Inverse relationship between engine speed and torque

data.title: Model T Car History
data.previewSummary: The Model T was the first affordable car, produced from 1908 to 1927, revolutionizing personal transportation.
previewBullets: Mass production made cars affordable for the average consumer
Standardized parts and interchangeable components simplified manufacturing
The Model T was the first car to be produced on an assembly line
data.details: The Model T was designed by Henry Ford with a focus on simplicity and affordability. It had a 2.9-liter engine and a 2-speed manual transmission. The car was produced on an assembly line, reducing production time and costs. The Model T was marketed as a reliable and durable vehicle, with a price point of around $825.
applications: Personal transportation for the average consumer
data.analogy: The Model T is like the first smartphone, making a previously luxury item accessible to the masses.
corePrinciples: Standardization of parts and manufacturing processes
mechanism: Henry Ford designed the Model T with simplicity and affordability in mind
The assembly line production process reduced production time and costs
The Model T was marketed as a reliable and durable vehicle
examples: The Ford Motor Company produced over 15 million Model T cars during its production run
The Model T was widely used for personal transportation and rural delivery services
misconceptions: The Model T was the first car ever produced
The Model T was only produced in black
constraints: Limited engine power and transmission options
formalStructure: Linear relationship between production costs and car prices
Exponential growth in car sales due to affordability
Threshold effect of mass production on car prices

data.title: Diesel Engine Basics
data.previewSummary: Internal combustion engine using diesel fuel for efficient power generation.
previewBullets: High compression ratio for ignition
Fuel injection for precise combustion
Efficient energy conversion for power output
data.details:
Diesel engines use a high compression ratio to ignite fuel, eliminating the need for spark plugs. Fuel is injected into the cylinder at the correct time for efficient combustion. This process results in a more efficient energy conversion and higher power output compared to gasoline engines.

Key advantages include increased torque, improved fuel efficiency, and reduced emissions.
data.analogy: A diesel engine is like a well-tuned orchestra, where each component works together in harmony to produce a powerful and efficient performance.
corePrinciples: High compression ratio for efficient ignition
Precise fuel injection for optimal combustion
Energy conversion efficiency for maximum power output
mechanism: Air is compressed to high pressure
Fuel is injected into the cylinder
Ignition occurs, producing power output
misconceptions: Diesel engines are only for heavy-duty use
Diesel engines are less efficient than gasoline engines
constraints: Higher compression ratio requires stronger engine block
Fuel injection system requires precise timing and control
formalStructure: Inverse relationship between compression ratio and ignition temperature
Exponential increase in power output with increasing compression ratio

data.title: Rotary Engine Basics
data.previewSummary: A type of internal combustion engine with a rotating combustion chamber.
previewBullets: Uses a rotor instead of a piston
Combustion occurs in a chamber attached to the rotor
High power-to-weight ratio
data.details:
Rotary engines use a rotor instead of a piston to generate power. The rotor has a combustion chamber attached to it, where fuel is ignited to produce power. This design allows for a high power-to-weight ratio, making it suitable for high-performance applications.
Key benefits include increased power density and reduced vibration.
data.analogy: Imagine a spinning top with a combustion chamber attached to it.
mechanism: Fuel-air mixture is drawn into the combustion chamber
Ignition occurs, producing a small explosion
The rotor spins, generating power
misconceptions: Rotary engines are less efficient than traditional engines
Rotary engines are only used in high-performance applications
constraints: Higher emissions compared to traditional engines
formalStructure: Non-linear relationship between rotor speed and power output
Exponential decay of fuel efficiency with increasing speed

data.title: Mazda RX Low Efficiency
data.previewSummary: The Mazda RX-7 has a low efficiency due to its rotary engine design.
previewBullets: Rotary engine design is less efficient than traditional piston engines
Low compression ratio and high oil consumption
Poor fuel economy and high emissions
data.details:
The rotary engine in the Mazda RX-7 has a low compression ratio, which leads to poor fuel economy and high emissions. Additionally, the engine's design requires more oil consumption, further reducing efficiency.
This results in lower fuel efficiency compared to traditional piston engines.
data.analogy: A rotary engine is like a car with a leaky tire, it's always losing energy.
corePrinciples: Energy loss due to friction and heat
mechanism: Fuel is burned, releasing energy
Energy is transferred to the rotor
Energy is lost due to friction and heat
misconceptions: Rotary engines are more powerful than piston engines
Rotary engines are more efficient than piston engines
constraints: Limited by energy loss due to friction and heat
data.problemPatterns: Low efficiency, high emissions, poor fuel economy
formalStructure: Inverse relationship between efficiency and energy loss
Exponential decay of energy due to friction and heat

data.title: Turbo Prop Engines
data.previewSummary: High-speed aircraft engines using turbine-driven propellers for efficiency and speed.
previewBullets: Combines turbine and propeller for efficiency
High-speed aircraft propulsion system
Improved fuel efficiency and speed
data.details:
Turbo prop engines use a turbine to drive a propeller, providing high-speed efficiency. They combine the benefits of turbines and propellers, offering improved fuel efficiency and speed compared to traditional propeller engines. Key components include the turbine, compressor, and propeller.
Key advantages include reduced fuel consumption and increased speed.
However, they are more complex and expensive than traditional engines.
data.analogy: A high-speed fan in a vacuum cleaner, but instead of sucking up dirt, it pushes air to generate thrust.
mechanism: Air enters the compressor, increasing pressure and temperature
Fuel is added, igniting and expanding gases
Turbine extracts energy from expanding gases, driving the propeller
misconceptions: Turbo prop engines are only for small aircraft
They are less efficient than jet engines
formalStructure: Linear relationship between turbine speed and propeller speed
Exponential decay of fuel consumption with increasing speed
Inverse relationship between engine weight and efficiency
